title: The Space of Circular Planar Electrical Networks
overall_summary: The paper studies circular planar electrical networks through medial strand diagrams, response matrices, and explicit coordinate systems on the cells of the response-matrix space. Its central constructions assign to every strand matching a standard minimal network via Dyck tilings, then recover the conductances of that network from Pfaffian expressions in the response matrix using tripod grove variables. It also constructs B variables on the cells of a minimal strand diagram; conductances are biratios of adjacent B variables and the B variables mutate by the cube recurrence under Y-Delta moves. For the original matching-complex problem, the most relevant material is the paper’s use of strand matchings, crossing resolutions, canonical rectilinear representatives, biratio coordinates, and boundary-compatible degeneration of positive coordinates when local network pieces are resolved or transformed.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Background facts recalled in Sections 2.2 and 2.3: equivalence, minimality, and strand matchings
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a circular planar electrical network, i.e. a finite graph embedded in the disk or plane with a distinguished cyclically ordered set of boundary vertices called nodes, and with positive conductances on edges. Electrical transformations are the local moves shown in Figure 1 of the paper: deleting a dead branch, deleting a self-loop, combining series edges, combining parallel edges, and applying a Y-Delta transformation. Two networks are electrically equivalent, meaning they have the same response matrix, if and only if they can be obtained from one another by electrical transformations. A network is minimal, or reduced, if it has the fewest edges in its topological equivalence class; any two minimal equivalent networks are related using only Y-Delta moves. The medial graph of a network has one degree-4 vertex for each network edge, stubs at boundary nodes, and strands obtained by going straight through every medial vertex. A circular planar network is minimal if and only if its medial graph has no closed strands, no strand crossing itself, and no pair of strands crossing more than once. For a minimal network with n nodes, labeling the two stubs at node i by 2i-1 and 2i gives a fixed-point-free involution pi of {1,...,2n}, called the strand matching. Y-Delta moves preserve pi, so pi depends only on the topological equivalence class of a minimal network. Conversely, every fixed-point-free involution of {1,...,2n} is the strand matching of some circular planar electrical network with n nodes, possibly a cactus network in which boundary nodes may be glued or disconnected.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence and minimality assertions are recalled from Colin de Verdiere, Gitler, and Vertigan. The paper does not reprove the full theorem, but uses the following mechanisms. Electrical transformations preserve the response matrix, and the cited theorem says they generate all response-matrix equivalences. Minimality can be read from the medial graph because closed strands, self-crossings, and repeated crossings correspond to reducible local configurations; absence of these configurations is exactly reducedness. A Y-Delta move changes only a small triangular/star patch in the network and does not alter how strands connect boundary stubs, hence the strand matching is invariant. To realize an arbitrary fixed-point-free involution, put 2n points in generic position on a circle and connect paired points by chords; the chord diagram is used as the medial strand diagram, and coloring complementary regions alternately produces dual circular planar networks realizing the matching.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3 construction: standard networks from Dyck tilings and strand matchings
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For each fixed-point-free involution, or strand matching, on {1,...,2n}, the paper constructs a distinguished rectilinear strand diagram and an associated circular planar network called a standard network. The construction uses Dyck tilings. A Dyck path of order n is a simple random-walk path on Z starting at 0, staying nonnegative, and returning to 0 after 2n steps. If lambda and mu are Dyck paths with lambda below mu, the skew region lambda/mu may be tiled by fattened Dyck paths, called Dyck tiles, subject to a cover-inclusive condition: whenever two tiles are vertically adjacent, the horizontal span of the upper tile is contained in the horizontal span of the lower tile. Given such a Dyck tiling, the associated strand diagram is drawn by placing strand endpoints along the upper Dyck path, letting each tile contain two strands crossing once, and letting possible additional strands pass horizontally through the tile. Coloring the complementary regions alternately gives a primal network and a dual network. The strand-matching-to-Dyck-tiling construction starts from the empty matching and uses two inverse elementary operations: inserting a new strand with adjacent paired stubs, which corresponds to splitting the Dyck tiling along a column and inserting a tent into every tile crossing that column; and crossing a pair of adjacent previously uncrossed strands, replacing pairs (a,i) and (i+1,b), with a<i<i+1<b, by (a,i+1) and (i,b), which corresponds to adding a square Dyck tile. In the special case where the lower Dyck path is the minimal zigzag path, the upper path is maximal, and all tiles are single boxes, the construction gives the usual standard well-connected network.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The construction is inductive. The base case n=0 is the empty matching and empty Dyck tiling. Any nonempty matching can be deconstructed by finding a peak in the Dyck-path representation of the matching. Such a peak corresponds either to an adjacent matched pair, which is removed by deleting the pair and merging the surrounding columns, or to adjacent endpoints belonging to two crossing-capable strands, which is uncrossed by replacing (a,i+1),(i,b) with (a,i),(i+1,b). Reversing these deconstruction steps gives the two construction moves. The local rules inside a Dyck tile ensure that when a square is added, exactly one new strand crossing is introduced, and when a tent is inserted, all affected strands are extended without changing their relative crossing data except as prescribed. Coloring the cells of the resulting rectilinear strand diagram produces the primal and dual networks.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1: Pfaffian formula for tripod partition functions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a circular planar network with cyclically ordered nodes, response matrix L, and grove partition sums Z_tau. For a partition tau of the nodes, Z_tau is the weighted sum over groves whose connected components induce tau, with weight the product of edge conductances. Let Z_uncrossing denote the grove partition sum for the partition in which every node is in a separate tree. Suppose tau is a tripod partition: the boundary can be divided into red, green, and blue arcs so that tau has one triple part and all remaining nonsingleton parts are parallel doubletons connecting nodes of different colors, with possible outer singleton parts at the color interfaces. Then Z_tau/Z_uncrossing is the Pfaffian of an explicitly defined skew-symmetric matrix constructed from L. In the color order red, then green, then blue, the ordinary rows and columns are indexed by the fully colored nodes and one additional row/column is appended. For two ordinary nodes i and j, the entry is 0 if they have the same color; it is L_ij for red-green and green-blue pairs; and, with the paper’s cyclic sign convention, it is -L_ij for red-blue pairs, extended skew-symmetrically below the diagonal. The last column is obtained, for each ordinary row i, by summing the positive ordinary entries in that row and adding the response-matrix term involving the singleton node, if present, whose two colors have no color in common with the color of i. With this convention, Pf of this matrix equals Z_tau/Z_uncrossing.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof reduces the statement to an earlier Pfaffian formula from Kenyon-Wilson for dual-tripod partitions in the dual network, expressed in terms of pairwise effective resistances. First assume tau has three singleton boundary nodes, one at each color interface. Then the dual partition tau* is a pairing of dual nodes, and planar duality gives Z_tau/Z_uncrossing = Z^*_tau*/Z^*_tree, where Z^*_tree is the weighted tree sum in the dual network. Apply the earlier dual-tripod formula in the dual network, where a formal variable t appears and the desired quantity is the coefficient of t in a Pfaffian. Index primal and dual nodes so that dual node i lies between primal nodes i and i+1. Perform Pfaffian-preserving operations subtracting each row from the preceding row and each column from the preceding column. Away from block boundaries, entries of the form -1/2 R^*_{i,j} + 1/2 R^*_{i+1,j} + 1/2 R^*_{i,j+1} - 1/2 R^*_{i+1,j+1} occur. These simplify to -L_ij using the dual-resistance/primal-response identity R^*_{i,j} = sum L_{i',j'} over cyclically ordered indices i'<=i<j'<=j. After these operations, t appears in only two positions. Extracting the linear coefficient in t expresses the answer as a signed sum of two Pfaffians. Moving one row/column to the last position and combining the two Pfaffians gives the stated single Pfaffian, and further row/column sign changes put it into the symmetric red-green-blue form described. If tau lacks one or more singleton interface nodes, add isolated boundary nodes with zero response entries; neither Z_tau nor Z_uncrossing changes, and the same Pfaffian formula applies.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2: uniqueness of comb groves in standard networks and their extensions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a standard minimal network obtained from the Dyck-tiling construction, and let chi be a crossing in its rectilinear strand diagram. Define the comb at chi as follows: take the strand segment going upward-left from chi as the spine, and at every crossing on this spine take the upward-right strand segment as a tooth. Resolve every crossing in this comb vertically and every crossing outside the comb horizontally; this determines a partition tau_chi of the nodes of G and a dual partition tau^*_chi. Also let tau_- be the all-horizontal partition. Let G~_chi be the enlarged network obtained by extending the strand diagram and inserting the notch above the top tooth of the comb so that the corresponding partition tau~_chi is a tripod or dual-tripod partition; let tau~_- be the all-horizontal partition in G~_chi. Then in G~_chi there is a unique grove of type tau~_chi and a unique grove of type tau~_-. In G there is a unique grove of type tau_chi and a unique grove of type tau_-, and these groves extend uniquely to the corresponding groves in G~_chi.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If tau~_chi is not a tripod partition in the primal network, pass to the dual network, where it is a tripod partition. Consider the grove drawn by taking vertical edges at comb crossings and horizontal edges elsewhere. The triple part of the tripod separates the disk into three regions. In each region, the partition pairs boundary nodes in nested parallel fashion. Starting from the outermost pair and moving inward toward the triple part, each connection in the displayed grove is forced to be as far from the triple part as possible; planarity leaves no room to reroute any of these pairings without changing the partition. Thus the grove of type tau~_chi is unique. The same forcing argument applies to the all-horizontal partition tau~_-. Finally, if G itself had two distinct groves inducing tau_chi or tau_-, either one could be spliced into the fixed outside part of G~_chi, producing two distinct groves of type tau~_chi or tau~_-, contradicting uniqueness in the extension.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3: tripod variables are monomials and conductances are biratios
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a standard minimal circular planar network, with rectilinear strand diagram coming from a Dyck tiling. For every crossing chi of the strand diagram, let tau_chi be the comb partition defined by resolving the comb at chi vertically and all other crossings horizontally, and include also the exterior all-horizontal partition tau_-. The associated tripod variables are the partition sums Z_tau for tau in {tau_-} union {tau_chi : chi a crossing}. Then the number of tripod variables is one more than the number of edges of G. Each tripod variable is a monomial, namely the product of the conductances of the edges in the unique grove of its partition type. For any crossing e, let c_e be its vertical conductance, meaning the conductance of the primal edge or dual edge that is vertical at that crossing. Let a be the first crossing on the strand going upward-left from e, let b be the first crossing on the strand going upward-right from e, and let f be the first crossing on the strand going upward-left from b. If a, b, or f does not exist, replace the corresponding tau_a, tau_b, or tau_f by tau_-. Then c_e = (Z_tau_e Z_tau_f)/(Z_tau_a Z_tau_b). Equivalently, using the corresponding dual partition sums, c_e = (Z^*_{tau^*_e} Z^*_{tau^*_f})/(Z^*_{tau^*_a} Z^*_{tau^*_b}).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Each edge of a standard minimal network corresponds to exactly one strand crossing, so the variables consist of one variable per edge plus the exterior variable. Lemma 2 gives uniqueness of the grove of every comb partition and of the exterior partition; hence each partition sum is the product of edge conductances in that unique grove. Compare the comb based at e with the comb based at a. The comb at e has exactly one additional tooth, starting at e, relative to the comb at a; therefore Z_tau_e/Z_tau_a is the product of all vertical conductances along the upward-right strand segment starting at e. Similarly, Z_tau_b/Z_tau_f is the product of all vertical conductances along the same upward-right strand segment after the first crossing b. Dividing these two ratios cancels every factor except the vertical conductance at e, giving the biratio formula. The dual formula follows because for every relevant partition tau, the dual grove weight satisfies Z^*_{tau^*} = Z_tau divided by the product of all primal edge weights, so the global factors cancel in the same biratio.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3.1 reconstruction consequence: conductances as Pfaffian rational functions of the response matrix
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a standard minimal network G, the conductance of every edge can be computed explicitly as a rational function of the response matrix L. For each crossing chi, enlarge G to the network G~_chi used in Lemma 2, in which the comb partition becomes a tripod or dual-tripod partition. The response matrix of such an enlargement can be obtained from L by a sequence of elementary response-matrix operations: adjoining an edge of conductance c between adjacent boundary nodes i and i+1 adds c to the off-diagonal entries L_{i,i+1}=L_{i+1,i} and subtracts c from L_{i,i} and L_{i+1,i+1}; inserting an isolated new node inserts a zero row and zero column; and internalizing a boundary node is performed by Schur reduction. Then the ratios Z_tau_e/Z_tau_a and Z_tau_f/Z_tau_b appearing in Theorem 3 equal ratios of normalized tripod Pfaffians in the corresponding enlarged networks, so the biratio formula of Theorem 3 reconstructs all conductances from Pfaffians of matrices built from L.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix crossings e,a,b,f as in Theorem 3. The paper chooses the extensions so that G~_a = G~_e and G~_f = G~_b. By Lemma 2, for any choice of conductances on the new edges in the extension, the new-edge contributions to the unique grove weights cancel in the ratio, and the normalizing factor Z_uncrossing cancels as well. Concretely, Z_tau_e/Z_tau_a = (Z_{tau~_e}(G~_e)/Z_unc(G~_e))/(Z_{tau~_a}(G~_a)/Z_unc(G~_a)), and similarly Z_tau_f/Z_tau_b = (Z_{tau~_f}(G~_f)/Z_unc(G~_f))/(Z_{tau~_b}(G~_b)/Z_unc(G~_b)). Each normalized extended partition function is a tripod or dual-tripod quantity, so Proposition 1 and its dual-tripod counterpart compute it as a Pfaffian in the response matrix of the extension. The extension response matrices are obtained from L by the three explicit operations above. Substituting these Pfaffian ratios into c_e = (Z_tau_e Z_tau_f)/(Z_tau_a Z_tau_b) gives the desired rational formula. The paper notes that computations are often simplified by taking the limiting case in which the newly added edges have infinite conductance.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4: B variables satisfy a conductance biratio formula
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be any minimal circular planar network, and draw its minimal strand diagram so that every strand (i,j), with i<j, moves monotonically from left to right. For a crossing between strands (i1,j1) and (i2,j2) with i1<i2<j1<j2, call the region between boundary nodes i1 and i2 the left region of that crossing. The horizontal conductance of the crossing is the conductance of the primal edge or dual edge whose endpoints lie in the left and right regions of the crossing. For each cell C of the strand diagram, corresponding to either a primal vertex or a primal face, define B_C to be the product of the horizontal conductances of all crossings whose left region contains C. Then for every primal edge e with endpoints v and v' and adjacent faces f and f', its conductance satisfies c(e) = B_v B_v'/(B_f B_f').
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix a crossing e and examine how its horizontal conductance contributes to the biratio B_v B_v'/(B_f B_f') at another crossing e'. If e' lies strictly inside the shadow, i.e. the left region, of e, then all four adjacent cells at e' contain the factor from e, so it cancels. If e' lies outside the shadow, none of the four B variables contains that factor. If e' lies on the boundary of the shadow but is not e itself, exactly two adjacent cells contain the factor, and those two factors again cancel between numerator and denominator. The only non-canceling contribution occurs when e'=e; there the biratio contributes exactly the conductance of the edge at e. Multiplying over all crossings gives the formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3.3 observation: B variables transform by the cube recurrence under Y-Delta moves
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a minimal network equipped with B variables as in Proposition 4, a local Y-Delta transformation changes the B variables by the cube recurrence. In the local notation of Figure 11, where B0 is the central B variable on one side of the move, B7 is the new central B variable after the move, and B1,...,B6 are the six surrounding B variables, the relation is B0 B7 = B1 B4 + B2 B5 + B3 B6. The surrounding variables are unchanged by the local move.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper records this as a property of B variables and points to the cube recurrence. The derivation is local: express the three conductances in the Y configuration as biratios of the adjacent B variables using Proposition 4, apply the standard Y-Delta conductance transformation from Figure 1, then require the three conductances in the Delta configuration to be expressible by the same biratio rule with one unknown central variable B7. Solving the resulting equations gives exactly B0 B7 = B1 B4 + B2 B5 + B3 B6.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5: contiguous minors as Laurent polynomials from truncated Aztec diamonds
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be an n by n matrix. For cyclic row and column indices, define a contiguous minor CMa,b,y(M) to be det M^{b+y-1,...,b}_{a,a+1,...,a+y-1}, i.e. the determinant with y consecutive rows and y consecutive columns, with the columns listed in reverse cyclic order as in the paper. Define the central minor CMx,y(M) by choosing a = floor((x-y)/2) and b = floor((x-y+n-(n-1 mod 2))/2), with indices interpreted modulo n. Let TADx,y,ell,n be the order-ell Aztec diamond centered at (x,y), truncated to use only squares with vertical coordinate between 0 and n. Assign to each horizontal domino centered at (p,q), with p integer and q half-integer, weight 1/(v_{p,q-1/2} v_{p,q+1/2}), and to each vertical domino centered at (p,q), with p half-integer and q integer, weight 1/(v_{p-1/2,q} v_{p+1/2,q}). Let P(TADx,y,ell,n) be the weighted sum of domino tilings multiplied by the monomial product of the variables v_{p,q} in the truncated diamond. If every variable v_{p,q} is specialized to the central minor CMp,q(M), then P(TADx,y,ell,n) equals a contiguous minor of M. More precisely, along the horizontal span of the truncated Aztec diamond, the rightmost central minor has either a row set or a column set different from the corresponding set of the adjacent central minor to its left; the leftmost central minor has the opposite type of changed set. The resulting contiguous minor uses the row set from the rightmost central minor and the column set from the leftmost central minor, or with row and column interchanged in the opposite orientation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof combines Kuo graphical condensation for domino tilings with the Desnanot-Jacobi determinant identity. For determinants, Desnanot-Jacobi gives a three-term recurrence relating a contiguous minor, the corresponding smaller minor, and four neighboring minors obtained by shifting row or column intervals. For weighted Aztec diamonds, Kuo condensation gives W(AD_{x,y,ell}) W(AD_{x,y,ell-2}) equal to the sum of two products: the up/down pair multiplied by the two horizontal boundary domino weights, plus the left/right pair multiplied by the two vertical boundary domino weights. Truncation is handled by setting weights of forbidden vertical boundary dominos to 0 and appropriate horizontal boundary weights to 1. With the special weights 1/(v v) and after multiplying by the monomial factors defining P, the explicit boundary weights cancel, leaving the clean recurrence P(TAD_{x,y,ell,n}) P(TAD_{x,y,ell-2,n}) = P(TAD_{x,y+1,ell-1,n}) P(TAD_{x,y-1,ell-1,n}) + P(TAD_{x-1,y,ell-1,n}) P(TAD_{x+1,y,ell-1,n}). The Laurent polynomials P and the claimed contiguous minors therefore satisfy the same recurrence. The base case ell=0 is tautological because P is just one central minor, and the case ell=1 is checked directly by Desnanot-Jacobi. Induction on ell proves the theorem.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 6: matrix entries are positive Laurent polynomials in central minors
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any n by n matrix M, each individual entry M_{i,j} can be expressed as a Laurent polynomial in the central minors CMx,y(M), and all coefficients in this Laurent polynomial are positive.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Theorem 5 with y=1 and choose x and ell so that the resulting contiguous minor has the single row i and single column j. The associated truncated Aztec-diamond Laurent polynomial is a sum over domino tilings with positive coefficients and monomial weights, so the expression has positive coefficients.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 7: disjoint contiguous minors are positive Laurent polynomials in small central minors
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be an n by n matrix. The small central minors are CMx,y(M) with 1 <= y < n/2, together with those with y = n/2 and x+y odd; in the nonsymmetric case x ranges over 1,...,2n. If a contiguous minor of M has disjoint row and column index sets, then it can be expressed as a Laurent polynomial with positive coefficients in the small central minors of M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use Theorem 5 to express the given contiguous minor as P(TADx,y,ell,n) for a suitable truncated Aztec diamond. If the row and column intervals are disjoint, one can take ell at most floor(n/2)-y, with the extreme case occurring when the intervals are adjacent. Thus the variables occurring in the truncated diamond have height at most floor(n/2). For odd n all central minors up to that height are small. For even n, the central minors at height n/2 that are not classified as small correspond to overlapping row and column sets, and hence do not appear in the disjoint contiguous-minor situation. The domino-tiling expansion supplies positivity of coefficients.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 8: arbitrary disjoint minors are Laurent polynomials in small central minors
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any n by n matrix M, any minor whose row index set and column index set are disjoint can be expressed as a Laurent polynomial in the small central minors of M. Positivity of coefficients is not asserted for arbitrary noncontiguous minors in this corollary.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A determinant is a polynomial in its matrix entries. If the row and column sets of the minor are disjoint, every matrix entry appearing in the determinant is off-diagonal. By Corollary 6, and in the required disjoint cases by the small-minor control of Corollary 7, these off-diagonal entries are Laurent polynomials in the small central minors. Substituting these Laurent expressions into the determinant expansion gives a Laurent polynomial in the small central minors.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 9: noninterlaced minors are positive rational functions of small central minors
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be an n by n matrix, with row and column indices arranged cyclically. A minor is noninterlaced if its row set and column set have the same cardinality and lie in noninterlacing cyclic intervals. Then every noninterlaced minor of M can be written as a positive rational function of the small central minors of M, meaning an expression formed from small central minors using addition, multiplication, and division with positive coefficients.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The key step is a determinant identity called the jaw move. Starting from the elementary identity for a matrix A with two equal rows, 0 = det of a 3 by 3 matrix whose first two rows are both indexed by z and whose third row is indexed by d, and columns are a,b,c, one applies Jacobi’s complementary minor identity after setting A generically equal to M^{-1}. This yields an identity of the form det M_b^b det M_{...}^{...,a,c} = det M_a^b det M_{...}^{...,b,c} + det M_c^b det M_{...}^{...,a,b}, with the exact deleted-row/deleted-column notation as in the paper. The identity expresses one noninterlaced determinant as a positive rational combination of simpler noninterlaced determinants. For a noninterlaced minor with at least one isolated node interspersed between the two sides, choose b to be such an isolated node, choose a and c to be the first and last nodes on the same side as b, and choose d to be an endpoint on the other side. With this choice, the jaw move either decreases the number of strands in the minor or keeps that number fixed while decreasing the number of interspersed isolated nodes. Repeatedly applying the move reduces any noninterlaced minor to noninterlaced contiguous minors. Corollary 7 expresses those contiguous minors as positive Laurent polynomials in small central minors, so the original minor is a positive rational function of small central minors.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 10: symmetric noninterlaced minors need only half the central minors
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a symmetric n by n matrix. Then every noninterlaced minor of M is a positive rational function of the small central minors CMx,y(M) with 1 <= x <= n, using the symmetric convention that identifies central minors related by reversing row and column roles.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For odd n, symmetry gives CMx+n,y = CMx,y, so Theorem 9 immediately reduces the needed variables to representatives with 1 <= x <= n. For even n, the only issue is that the minimally off-center contiguous minors of height n/2 occur in opposite pairs, and only one member of each pair is included among the chosen small central minors. A condensation identity expresses the omitted off-center contiguous minor as a positive rational expression involving its opposite included off-center minor and central contiguous minors. After this replacement, the proof of Theorem 9 uses only the chosen half-set of small central minors.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 11: positivity of small central response minors implies well-connectedness
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a circular planar network with n nodes and response matrix L. If each of the binomial(n,2) small central minors of L, in the symmetric sense of Theorem 10, is positive, then G is well-connected. Here well-connected means equivalently that for every pair of noninterlaced node subsets A and B of the same size, there are pairwise vertex-disjoint paths connecting A to B in pairs, or equivalently that all noninterlaced minors of L are strictly positive.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Theorem 10, every noninterlaced minor of the symmetric response matrix L is a positive rational function of the small central minors. If all small central minors are positive, all noninterlaced minors are therefore positive. The Colin de Verdiere criterion recalled in the paper says that positivity of all noninterlaced response minors is equivalent to well-connectedness of the circular planar network.

proof_techniques.name: Medial strand matching as a combinatorial invariant
proof_techniques.description: The paper systematically replaces a circular planar network by its medial strand diagram. Minimality becomes a purely diagrammatic condition: no closed strands, no self-crossing strands, and no pair of strands crossing twice. The boundary behavior of the strands is encoded by a fixed-point-free involution of boundary stubs, and Y-Delta transformations preserve this involution. Thus a large geometric/electrical equivalence problem is reduced to manipulating planar matchings of strand endpoints.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original CW complex is built from crossing matchings and their local resolutions. The medial-strand viewpoint suggests trying to identify invariants of matchings under local moves and to characterize which crossing diagrams are reduced or canonical using only forbidden local strand configurations.

proof_techniques.name: Canonical rectilinear representative via Dyck tilings
proof_techniques.description: For every strand matching, the paper constructs a standard rectilinear strand diagram using Dyck tilings. The construction is recursive and uses two elementary operations: adding a local crossing of adjacent strands and inserting a new adjacent matched pair by splitting the tiling. This gives a controlled representative in which directions such as upward-left and upward-right are meaningful and local combs can be defined.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A proof of contractibility for a matching complex may benefit from assigning a canonical representative or normal form to every isotopy class. The Dyck-tiling recursion is a candidate template for inducting on boundary pairs or crossings while preserving a planar matching structure.

proof_techniques.name: Comb resolutions and unique monomial witnesses
proof_techniques.description: In a standard network, a crossing determines a comb-shaped subset of the strand diagram. Resolving comb crossings vertically and all other crossings horizontally gives a boundary partition whose grove is unique. Therefore the corresponding partition function is not a complicated sum but a single monomial in conductances. Comparing neighboring combs makes almost all factors cancel and leaves one local conductance as a biratio.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original cells are cubes whose faces are crossing resolutions. The comb construction suggests looking for distinguished resolution patterns with uniqueness or forcing properties, then using cancellation between adjacent patterns to build inductive contractions or coordinates on cells.

proof_techniques.name: Biratio coordinates on vertices and faces
proof_techniques.description: The B-variable construction assigns a product of horizontal conductances to every cell of a minimal strand diagram. Each conductance is recovered as the cross ratio B_v B_v'/(B_f B_f') of the two endpoint-vertex variables and two adjacent-face variables. The proof is purely combinatorial: for any other crossing, its contribution to the cross ratio cancels unless it is the crossing being measured.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The cancellation-by-regions argument is potentially portable to any planar crossing complex with local face/region data. It may help define a height function, potential, or discrete Morse matching by assigning multiplicative or additive weights to regions and showing local crossing changes affect only one factor.

proof_techniques.name: Cube recurrence for local mutations
proof_techniques.description: Under a Y-Delta move, B variables transform by the cube recurrence B0 B7 = B1 B4 + B2 B5 + B3 B6. This packages a local planar transformation into a positive algebraic mutation, while keeping the surrounding variables fixed. It is a compatibility statement between local cell changes and global coordinates.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The cells of F_w are products of intervals indexed by crossings, and facets are local resolutions. The cube recurrence is a model for proving that different orders of local transformations are compatible, which is often the key obstruction in showing that a cubical or CW complex is a ball or contractible.

proof_techniques.name: Boundary-compatible conductance degeneration
proof_techniques.description: The paper’s coordinates are designed so that moving from one cell of the response-matrix space to a boundary cell corresponds to a conductance tending to 0 or infinity. Although the regularity of the full Lam-Pylyavskyy cell complex is stated as a conjectural/topological context in the introduction, the explicit coordinate formulas show how local network degenerations are reflected by vanishing or diverging coordinate factors.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original attaching maps are precisely given by resolving one crossing at a time. A plausible strategy is to realize F_w, or a closely related complex, as a boundary stratification of a contractible positive parameter space where facets are reached by one coordinate going to an endpoint.

proof_techniques.name: Pfaffian evaluation of planar boundary partition functions
proof_techniques.description: Tripod and dual-tripod grove partition functions are computed as Pfaffians of matrices made from the response matrix. The proof uses planar duality, an effective-resistance formula in the dual network, Pfaffian-preserving row/column operations, and extraction of a coefficient in an auxiliary variable. The result turns certain planar connectivity sums into explicit algebraic coordinates.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem has inverse-letter pairing constraints around complementary regions. Pfaffian formulas are a natural tool for planar pairing enumerations and may help encode allowed resolution states or count/weight them in a way that behaves well under local uncrossing.

proof_techniques.name: Condensation and positive Laurent expansions
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 5 proves Laurent expansions for contiguous minors by matching the Desnanot-Jacobi recurrence for determinants with Kuo condensation for domino tilings of truncated Aztec diamonds. The result is a positive Laurent phenomenon: complicated minors are represented by sums over tilings, and the recurrence proves consistency.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the matching complex admits recursive decompositions by resolving local crossings, an analogous condensation recurrence could prove that different recursive reductions are compatible or that certain intervals in the face poset have positive tiling models, supporting shellability or contractibility arguments.

proof_techniques.name: Jaw-move induction
proof_techniques.description: The proof of positivity for noninterlaced minors uses a local determinant identity, the jaw move, to reduce a noncontiguous noninterlaced configuration to simpler ones. The complexity measure is lexicographic: first the number of strands, then the number of interspersed isolated nodes. Repetition reduces to contiguous cases already controlled by Laurent expansions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The quasi-reduced word condition in the original problem may allow a similar complexity-reducing local move: choose an isolated or extremal boundary feature and express the global complex in terms of simpler matching complexes, aiming for an inductive contractibility proof.

other_useful_info: The introduction recalls Lam and Pylyavskyy’s cell complex on the space Omega_n of response matrices: cells are parametrized by equivalence classes of minimal networks, and the cell dimension is the number of edges of a minimal representative. They conjectured that this is a regular CW complex whose attaching maps arise by sending a conductance to 0 or infinity; Lam later showed the cell complex is Eulerian. This is closely related to uncrossing partial orders on matchings; the paper cites Lam, 'The uncrossing partial order on matchings is Eulerian' [29]. After Theorem 3, the paper notes that the tripod variables satisfy one monomial relation: choosing a lowermost crossing chi, the tripod variable Z_{tau_chi} can be expressed as a Laurent monomial in the other tripod variables because the corresponding edge conductance appears in only one biratio. The paper states several conjectures that may be relevant as analogies: Conjecture 1 says that for a minimal network with m edges, positivity should be testable using m noninterlaced minors; Conjecture 2 predicts a base set S of m noninterlaced minors such that every nonzero noninterlaced minor is both Laurent and positive in S; Conjecture 3, later proved by Tri Lai according to the paper’s note, predicts domino-tiling Laurent formulas for semicontiguous minors. The final question asks for a decision procedure that, using at most binomial(n,2) positivity tests, determines the strand matching whose standard-network conductances are positive.
why_relevant: Develops coordinates and cell-structure compatibility for circular planar electrical networks. The degeneration of conductances to boundary cells parallels resolving crossings in a matching complex.
